title: Malnutrition and Nutritional Assessment
authors: Charles Chin Han Lew, Charlene W. Compher

items.subtopic: Malnutrition and Nutritional Assessment | MALNUTRITION DIAGNOSIS IN CLINICAL SETTINGS
items.title: Prevalence and Impact of Malnutrition in Hospitalized Patients
items.content: Malnutrition is prevalent among hospitalized patients, affecting 30–50% depending on various factors. It leads to significant adverse outcomes such as poor wound healing, compromised immune status, impaired organ function, increased hospital stay, readmissions, higher mortality, and elevated healthcare costs.
keyPoints: Prevalence of malnutrition in hospitalized patients: 30–50%
Adverse outcomes include poor wound healing, compromised immunity, impaired organ function
Consequences: Increased length of hospital stay, readmissions, higher mortality, increased healthcare costs
items.reference: Malnutrition and Nutritional Assessment · Malnutrition and Nutritional Assessment | MALNUTRITION DIAGNOSIS IN CLINICAL SETTINGS

items.subtopic: Malnutrition and Nutritional Assessment | MALNUTRITION DIAGNOSIS IN CLINICAL SETTINGS
items.title: GLIM Criteria for Diagnosing Malnutrition in Adults
items.content: The Global Leadership Initiative on Malnutrition (GLIM) criteria, introduced in 2019, provide a standardized approach to diagnosing malnutrition. It requires one etiologic criterion and one phenotypic criterion. The etiologic criteria include inflammation/disease burden or reduced food intake/assimilation, while the phenotypic criteria involve weight loss, low BMI, or reduced muscle mass.
keyPoints: GLIM introduced in 2019
Requires one etiologic (inflammation/disease burden or reduced food intake/assimilation) and one phenotypic criterion (weight loss, low BMI, or reduced muscle mass)
Facilitates diagnosis across settings with variable resources
items.reference: Malnutrition and Nutritional Assessment · Malnutrition and Nutritional Assessment | MALNUTRITION DIAGNOSIS IN CLINICAL SETTINGS

items.subtopic: NUTRITION ASSESSMENT FOR PROTEIN-CALORIE MALNUTRITION
items.title: Screening Tools for Malnutrition Risk in Hospital Settings
items.content: In hospital settings, malnutrition risk screening is crucial and typically performed by bedside nurses or clinical dietitians using validated tools. These tools include the Malnutrition Screening Tool, Malnutrition Universal Screening Tool, and Nutritional Risk Screening 2002, among others.
keyPoints: Use of validated tools like Malnutrition Screening Tool, Malnutrition Universal Screening Tool, Nutritional Risk Screening 2002 for screening malnutrition risk.
items.reference: Malnutrition and Nutritional Assessment · NUTRITION ASSESSMENT FOR PROTEIN-CALORIE MALNUTRITION

items.subtopic: NUTRITION ASSESSMENT FOR PROTEIN-CALORIE MALNUTRITION
items.title: GLIM Criteria Feasibility in Outpatient Settings
items.content: In outpatient settings where dietitian support may be limited, the GLIM criteria can still be applied. Nurses can measure height and weight for BMI calculation and assess muscle mass using calf or arm circumference measurements to diagnose malnutrition.
keyPoints: GLIM criteria are feasible in outpatient settings.
Nurses can use height, weight (for BMI), and calf/arm circumference to assess malnutrition.
items.reference: Malnutrition and Nutritional Assessment · NUTRITION ASSESSMENT FOR PROTEIN-CALORIE MALNUTRITION

items.subtopic: ■ COMPONENTS OF A DETAILED NUTRITIONAL ASSESSMENT (part 1)
items.title: Understanding Malnutrition Risk Based on Medical/Surgical History
items.content: Recognizing the risk of malnutrition is crucial and varies with disease type and progression. Acute inflammation can exacerbate chronic conditions, altering nutrient requirements and diminishing the effectiveness of nutritional interventions.
keyPoints: Malnutrition risk varies by medical/surgical diagnosis and clinical setting.
Acute inflammatory events superimposed on chronic conditions increase malnutrition risk.
Severe inflammation may reduce the benefit of nutritional interventions to being supportive rather than therapeutic.
items.reference: Malnutrition and Nutritional Assessment · ■ COMPONENTS OF A DETAILED NUTRITIONAL ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ COMPONENTS OF A DETAILED NUTRITIONAL ASSESSMENT (part 1)
items.title: Significance of Nonvolitional Weight Loss in Malnutrition
items.content: Nonvolitional weight loss is a critical indicator of underlying disease or inflammation. The degree and duration of this weight loss are key to assessing its clinical significance.
keyPoints: 5–10% nonvolitional weight loss over 6 months is clinically relevant.
>10% nonvolitional weight loss over the same period is considered severe.
items.reference: Malnutrition and Nutritional Assessment · ■ COMPONENTS OF A DETAILED NUTRITIONAL ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ COMPONENTS OF A DETAILED NUTRITIONAL ASSESSMENT (part 1)
items.title: BMI and Malnutrition: Beyond Obesity
items.content: While BMI is a tool for screening overweight or obesity, it does not fully capture malnutrition risk. Patients of any BMI can be malnourished due to factors like muscle mass loss.
keyPoints: BMI categories: <18.5 kg/m² = underweight; 18.5–24.9 kg/m² = desirable; 25.0–29.9 kg/m² = overweight; ≥30 kg/m² = obese.
Overweight or obese patients can be malnourished due to weight loss or muscle mass loss (sarcopenic obesity).
Being underweight does not automatically indicate malnutrition.
items.reference: Malnutrition and Nutritional Assessment · ■ COMPONENTS OF A DETAILED NUTRITIONAL ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ COMPONENTS OF A DETAILED NUTRITIONAL ASSESSMENT (part 2)
items.title: Role of Laboratory Indicators in Diagnosing Malnutrition
items.content: Laboratory indicators such as albumin and prealbumin have poor sensitivity and specificity for diagnosing malnutrition due to their reduction during systemic responses to injury, disease, or inflammation. C-reactive protein is a more reliable indicator of inflammation, with trends over time clarifying the inflammatory challenge.
keyPoints: No single biomarker describes comprehensive nutritional status.
Albumin and prealbumin are negative acute-phase reactants with poor sensitivity and specificity for malnutrition.
C-reactive protein can help identify inflammation; trends in levels provide insight into the extent of inflammation.
items.reference: Malnutrition and Nutritional Assessment · ■ COMPONENTS OF A DETAILED NUTRITIONAL ASSESSMENT (part 2)

items.subtopic: ■ COMPONENTS OF A DETAILED NUTRITIONAL ASSESSMENT (part 2)
items.title: Importance of Diet History and Nutritional Intake Assessment
items.content: Diet history should be assessed to understand food patterns and nutrient intake, often requiring consultation with a clinical dietitian. Adequate assessment includes all forms of nutrition support—oral, enteral, and parenteral—and ongoing reassessment is crucial for optimizing patient care.
keyPoints: Brief diet surveys target specific dietary aspects but require detailed evaluation by a dietitian.
Assessment must include oral supplements, enteral tube feedings, and parenteral nutrition.
Ongoing reassessment of nutritional intake is essential during hospitalization.
items.reference: Malnutrition and Nutritional Assessment · ■ COMPONENTS OF A DETAILED NUTRITIONAL ASSESSMENT (part 2)

items.subtopic: ■ COMPONENTS OF A DETAILED NUTRITIONAL ASSESSMENT (part 2)
items.title: Functional Outcomes in Malnutrition
items.content: Advanced malnutrition leads to declines in muscle mass and function, detectable by functional tests like handgrip strength. This test is clinically useful for identifying patients who benefit most from nutritional support.
keyPoints: Malnutrition results in decreased muscle mass and function.
Handgrip strength is a practical test for assessing muscle function and guiding nutritional interventions.
items.reference: Malnutrition and Nutritional Assessment · ■ COMPONENTS OF A DETAILED NUTRITIONAL ASSESSMENT (part 2)

items.question: Which statement about the reliability of historic nutrition parameters in malnourished patients with inflammation is correct?
options: Albumin and prealbumin are reliable indicators.
Inflammation does not affect albumin or prealbumin levels.

items.explanation: High levels of inflammation render albumin and prealbumin unreliable as nutrition indicators.
